input.prompt:
I'm building a photo viewer page in my .NET MAUI app targeting .NET 10. I want
the image to fill the entire screen edge-to-edge, going behind the status bar
and navigation bar. But my image still has padding around it even though I set
SafeAreaEdges="None" on the ContentPage. What am I missing?

input.initialPrompt:
I'm building a photo viewer page in my .NET MAUI app targeting .NET 10. I want
the image to fill the entire screen edge-to-edge, going behind the status bar
and navigation bar. But my image still has padding around it even though I set
SafeAreaEdges="None" on the ContentPage. What am I missing?

data.content:
`SafeAreaEdges="None"` only tells the page not to inset content away from system bars — it doesn't make the system bars transparent or hide app-level chrome. You likely need a combination of fixes:

**1. Hide the app navigation bar** (Shell or NavigationPage adds its own bar):

```xml
<!-- If using Shell -->
<ContentPage Shell.NavBarIsVisible="False"
             SafeAreaEdges="None" ...>

<!-- If using NavigationPage -->
<ContentPage NavigationPage.HasNavigationBar="False"
             SafeAreaEdges="None" ...>
```

**2. Remove any explicit page `Padding`:**

```xml
<ContentPage Padding="0" ...>
```

**3. Make Android system bars transparent** so content actually shows behind them. In `Platforms/Android/MainActivity.cs`:

```csharp
protected override void OnCreate(Bundle? savedInstanceState)
{
    base.OnCreate(savedInstanceState);

    Window.SetStatusBarColor(Android.Graphics.Color.Transparent);
    Window.SetNavigationBarColor(Android.Graphics.Color.Transparent);
}
```

> On .NET 10 targeting Android 15 (API 35), edge-to-edge is enforced and bars are transparent by default — but if you target a lower API, you need the above.

**4. On iOS**, `SafeAreaEdges="None"` should be sufficient. If you still see gaps, set `IgnoreSafeArea="True"` directly on the `Image`:

```xml
<Image Source="photo.jpg"
       Aspect="AspectFill"
       IgnoreSafeArea="True" />
```

**Minimal full example:**

```xml
<ContentPage xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/dotnet/2021/maui"
             Shell.NavBarIsVisible="False"
             SafeAreaEdges="None"
             Padding="0">
    <Image Source="photo.jpg"
           Aspect="AspectFill"
           IgnoreSafeArea="True" />
</ContentPage>
```

The most common culprit is #1 — the Shell/NavigationPage bar is still visible, which pushes everything down.
data.reasoningText:
The user is working with .NET MAUI and wants to achieve edge-to-edge (full-screen) image display, going behind the status bar and navigation bar. They've tried setting `SafeAreaEdges="None"` on the ContentPage but the image still has padding.

This is a .NET MAUI question about edge-to-edge/full-bleed content. Let me think about what's needed:

In .NET MAUI (.NET 9+), there's the concept of safe area handling. The `SafeAreaEdges` property controls which edges respect the safe area insets. Setting it to `None` should tell the page not to add safe area padding.


However, there could be a few culprits here. The issue might be that edge-to-edge mode isn't properly enabled on the platform level, or there could be additional padding coming from a parent Shell or NavigationPage wrapper. On Android specifically, edge-to-edge behavior changed starting with .NET 9 targeting Android 15, so depending on the target API level, you might need to explicitly configure it.

There's also the `IgnoreSafeArea` property on individual views that could help. The real solution in .NET 10 comes down to understanding `SafeAreaEdges` on ContentPage — setting it to "None" tells the page to extend content all the way to the edges. But the platform-level configuration matters too: on Android, the app needs to be drawing behind system bars, while iOS handles this more automatically.

For Android specifically, if you're targeting older APIs below Android 15, you might need to explicitly configure the window to not fit system windows. In .NET 9 and later, MAUI improved this handling significantly, though there are still some practical considerations to watch for — explicit padding on the page, layout margins, or opaque system bars can all interfere with true edge-to-edge rendering.

The most common culprits are usually the app's own navigation bar taking up space at the top, Android's system bars not being transparent even when content ignores safe areas, or default padding baked into the page itself. I should give them a straightforward solution that addresses these specific issues.
